Definition: Available student resources for obtaining meals
Interpretation: Represents the budget, time, and information capacity students can use for food decisions
Campus Food Example: Meal budget decreases after payment; available time decreases while waiting in queue

Definition: Student mission intensity for obtaining a suitable meal
Interpretation: Measures how strongly students need or intend to obtain meals at time t
Campus Food Example: Mission intensity peaks during lunch and exam week

Definition: Student-generated outputs to the ecosystem
Interpretation: Represents demand and knowledge signals produced by students
Campus Food Example: Students place orders and submit ratings

Definition: Value received by students from the food ecosystem
Interpretation: Captures health, satisfaction, convenience, affordability, and learning-readiness value
Campus Food Example: Higher nutrition and shorter waiting time increase student value

Definition: Inputs available for meal production
Interpretation: Represents ingredients, labor, kitchen capacity, and capital
Campus Food Example: Ingredients decrease as meals are produced and increase after supplier delivery

Definition: Vendor production and service mission intensity
Interpretation: Shows the percentage of vendor capacity planned for mission execution
Campus Food Example: Vendor operates at 90% capacity during lunch peak

Definition: Meals and services produced by the vendor
Interpretation: Represents the actual quantity of meals produced and service capacity delivered
Campus Food Example: Vendor produces 150 meals from available resources

Definition: Financial and non-financial value received by the vendor
Interpretation: Captures profit, business viability, reputation, and compliance effects
Campus Food Example: Profit increases when meals are sold and decreases when waste is high

Definition: Available supply inventory and logistics capacity
Interpretation: Represents stock and delivery capacity available to support vendors
Campus Food Example: Supplier stock decreases after delivering ingredients to vendors

Definition: Supplier mission intensity for fulfilling vendor demand
Interpretation: Measures planned supply commitment relative to capacity
Campus Food Example: Supplier increases mission intensity before exam week

Definition: Delivered ingredients and supply information
Unit: ingredient units, delivery count, information records
Interpretation: Represents useful supply outputs received by vendors
Campus Food Example: Supplier delivers vegetables and rice to canteen vendors

Definition: Supplier value from transactions and relationship reliability
Interpretation: Captures revenue, margin, and long-term relationship value
Campus Food Example: On-time delivery improves supplier reliability score

Definition: Delivery resources available for meal or supply movement
Unit: riders, vehicles, delivery slots, energy units
Interpretation: Represents logistics capacity and operational availability
Campus Food Example: Available riders decrease during lunch peak

Definition: Delivery mission intensity for fulfilling logistics demand
Interpretation: Measures logistics utilization and delivery commitment
Campus Food Example: Delivery intensity rises when online ordering increases

Definition: Completed deliveries and routing information
Interpretation: Represents delivered meals or supplies and logistics service output
Campus Food Example: Delivery actor completes 80 meal deliveries

Definition: Value received from delivery service
Interpretation: Captures income, reliability, and environmental cost
Campus Food Example: More efficient routing increases delivery value

Definition: Institutional resources for governing the ecosystem
Interpretation: Represents subsidy budget, facilities, data access, and governance authority
Campus Food Example: University allocates subsidy to improve meal affordability

Definition: University mission intensity for student welfare and campus sustainability
Interpretation: Measures institutional commitment to campus food mission
Campus Food Example: University increases governance intensity after food waste rises

Definition: Governance outputs produced by the university
Interpretation: Represents institutional interventions that shape the ecosystem
Campus Food Example: University issues a plastic reduction policy

Definition: Institutional and mission value generated by the food ecosystem
Interpretation: Captures campus welfare, reputation, mission achievement, and sustainability
Campus Food Example: Higher student satisfaction and lower waste increase university value

Definition: Digital Twin Mini-Lab and coordination resources
Unit: data records, compute units, model assets, staff-hours
Interpretation: Represents data, software, models, dashboards, and computational capacity
Campus Food Example: Operator receives demand data and uses compute resources to run MSRS

Definition: Operator mission intensity for monitoring, simulation, recommendation, and reporting
Interpretation: Measures how intensively the operator coordinates the ecosystem
Campus Food Example: Operator increases recommendation frequency during multi-shock scenario

Definition: Digital coordination outputs
Interpretation: Represents decision-support artifacts produced by the operator
Campus Food Example: PUDAL generates vendor production recommendations

Definition: Coordination value generated by the operator
Interpretation: Measures how much the digital coordination layer improves ecosystem performance
Campus Food Example: Operator value is high when MSRS reduces waste and shortage simultaneously

Definition: Regulatory authority and enforcement capacity
Interpretation: Represents rules, inspection capacity, standards, and enforcement resources
Campus Food Example: Regulator has limited inspection capacity per week

Definition: Regulatory mission intensity for safety, fairness, and sustainability compliance
Interpretation: Measures how strongly regulatory control is applied
Campus Food Example: Regulatory intensity rises after safety risk increases

Definition: Regulatory outputs
Interpretation: Represents compliance actions that shape stakeholder behavior
Campus Food Example: Regulator certifies vendors meeting hygiene standards

Definition: Public value from regulation
Unit: normalized trust, safety, and compliance score
Interpretation: Captures trust, safety, fairness, and environmental compliance
Campus Food Example: Food safety compliance improves public trust
